one_line_takeaway: Deep image-ranking / retrieval models are easily manipulated by imperceptible perturbations to either candidates or queries, and the paper's Anti-Collapse Triplet adversarial-training defense plus its Empirical Robustness Score give a 60%-540% robustness gain over the authors' earlier EST defense while generalizing better on clean data.
key_finding: Undefended deep ranking models are highly vulnerable: e.g., on MNIST a strong CA+ (w=1, eps=77/255) raises a candidate's mean rank percentile from 50% to 3.3% and CA- lowers it to 69.9%; on SOP CA+ (w=1) reaches 0.1% (near top) and CA- reaches 99.3% (near bottom). The proposed ACT defense substantially outperforms the prior EST defense on the ERS metric across all five datasets, achieving between 60% and 540% ERS improvement over EST, while also generalizing better on benign (unperturbed) examples -- especially under the zero-shot CUB/CARS settings. ACT is also the only method with consistently non-zero performance on the very hard GTT attack. The diagnostic mitigations confirm the analysis: REST (fixing only misleading gradients) alone yields 49%-402% ERS improvement over EST, and enlarging the triplet margin (fixing mini-batch exploitation) also raises ERS (Fashion: 36.4 -> up to 63.9).
core_contribution: A journal extension (of the authors' prior conference paper [13]) that studies adversarial attack, defense, and robustness evaluation for deep ranking / deep metric learning (DML) retrieval systems. The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) two families of adversarial ranking attacks -- Candidate Attack (CA+, CA-) which perturbs a candidate image to raise/lower its rank, and Query Attack (QA+, QA-, plus a Semantics-Preserving variant SP-QA) which perturbs the query image to raise/lower ranks of chosen candidates -- formulated by encoding the desired ranking order as inequalities, converting them into triplet-like losses, and solving with PGD; (2) a NEW defense, Anti-Collapse Triplet (ACT), the headline new contribution of this extension, in which positive and negative samples are adversarially 'collapsed' together and the model is trained to separate them, avoiding the misleading-gradient and inefficient-mini-batch-exploitation problems the authors diagnose in their earlier Embedding-Shifted Triplet (EST) defense; and (3) a NEW Empirical Robustness Score (ERS), an aggregate white-box robustness metric for ranking models that averages normalized scores over a representative attack suite. NOTE ON ATTRIBUTION: EST is from the authors' own conference version [13] (used here as the baseline/state-of-the-art ranking defense to beat), and several attacks in the ERS suite are external / cited work adapted as evaluation probes (TMA [39], LTM [40], DPQN [59]=ES, AdvPattern [7], Li et al. [38], QAIR [42] simplified into the paper's GTT); the genuinely new attacks introduced in this paper are GTM (Greedy Top-1 Misranking) and GTT (Greedy Top-1 Translocation, simplified from QAIR).
threat_model: White-box adversary against DNN-based image ranking / retrieval systems (e.g., e-commerce visual product search, face/person recognition). Perturbations are L-infinity-bounded (epsilon in {8/255, 77/255} for MNIST/Fashion; {2/255, 8/255} for CUB/CARS/SOP) and 'visually imperceptible'. Two access models: a malicious party who can modify a CANDIDATE image (e.g., a seller raising their own product rank via CA+ or demoting a competitor via CA-), and a man-in-the-middle who can modify the QUERY image (e.g., a malicious ad company promoting/impeding products via QA+/QA-). The attacker knows model parameters and gradients; the defender has zero prior knowledge of which attack will be used, motivating a generic defense and a multi-attack robustness score.
limitations: Robustness is purely EMPIRICAL (no certified guarantees) and evaluated only under WHITE-BOX attacks; transferable, universal, and black-box attacks are explicitly excluded from ERS (justified by the argument that resistance to simpler white-box attacks implies resistance to harder ones, which is an assumption rather than a proof). Adversarial training imposes a clear benign-accuracy cost (e.g., SOP R@1 drops from 62.9% undefended to 46.0% EST / 47.5% ACT; CUB from 53.9% to 8.5% EST / 27.5% ACT), and even ACT's absolute ERS remains modest on hard datasets (CUB 33.9, CARS 38.6). ERS aggregates heterogeneous attack scores into a single averaged number, so it can obscure per-attack weaknesses and its normalization choices are somewhat ad hoc. Experiments use small backbones (C2F2, ResNet-18/50) and academic retrieval benchmarks rather than production-scale retrieval systems; effects of loss function / distance metric choice are largely deferred to prior work and future study.

quantitative_results: Undefended MNIST C2F2: CA+ (w=1, eps=77/255) raises mean rank percentile from 50% to 3.3%; CA- raises it to 69.9%
Undefended SOP ResNet-18: CA+ (w=1) reaches 0.1% rank percentile (top); CA- reaches 99.3% (bottom)
ACT achieves 60% to 540% ERS improvement over EST across all datasets
REST (mitigating only misleading gradient) achieves 49% to 402% ERS improvement over EST
ACT is the only defense with consistently non-zero GTT success rate (e.g., MNIST 1.1%, SOP 15.3%)
PGD: eta=32 iterations, step alpha=max(1/255, round(eps*255/25)/255); embedding dim D=512, triplet margin beta=0.2
baselines_compared: Embedding-Shifted Triplet (EST) -- authors' prior conference-version defense [13], treated as state-of-the-art ranking defense baseline
Revised EST (REST) -- authors' variant fixing misleading gradients
EST with larger margin beta -- variant fixing inefficient mini-batch exploitation
Architecture ablations: C2F2, ResNet-18, ResNet-50, IBN-Net, MnasNet; semihard triplet sampling

Attack probes drawn from external work used inside ERS: TMA [39], LTM [40], ES/DPQN [59], AdvPattern [7], Li et al. [38], QAIR [42] (simplified to GTT); new attacks GTM and GTT introduced by this paper
